perturbation.original_text: 1.7 "Authorized Customer" means a Loop Party customer that is a party to a Transferred Contract, solely with respect to the Product quantities covered by the Transferred Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 "Authorized Customer" means any customer of a Loop Party, regardless of whether they are a party to a Transferred Contract, and includes potential customers who have expressed interest in purchasing Products.
perturbation.explanation: This change broadens the definition of "Authorized Customer" to include any customer of a Loop Party, even potential ones, contradicting the original definition which limited it to customers with a Transferred Contract. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 2.7 where the 'Marketing and Sale Restriction' relies on the original narrower definition of 'Authorized Customer'. The change introduces uncertainty in determining which customers the Joint Venture Company is restricted from marketing to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.7 Exclusivity. Joint Venture Company shall not, and shall cause each SPV and each of their Affiliates not to, directly or indirectly, market or sell any Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter to any person or entity except to Authorized Customers pursuant to a Transferred Contract (the "Marketing and Sale Restriction"); provided that if at any time during the term of this Agreement, Loop has delivered Proposed Contract Notices that result in Transferred Contracts having binding purchase and supply obligations for less than [***] of the then existing actual production capacity of a Licensed Facility (taking into account Joint Venture Company's reasonable forecasts based on customer requirements, seasonal variations, and other factors) in the subsequent three months (the "[***]"), Joint Venture Company may market and solicit orders for Licensed Products produced in such Licensed Facility directly to customers and potential customers and enter into supply contracts for the sale of Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter produced in such Licensed Facility with customers without being restricted by the Marketing and Sale Restriction. If at any subsequent time during the term of this Agreement Loop gives Joint Venture Party written notice that Loop is then capable of meeting, and is prepared to meet, the [***] and provides reasonable supporting information and documentation to Joint Venture Party with such notice, then at a date selected by Joint Venture Company that is within ninety (90) days after Joint Venture Company receives such written notice, the Market and Sale Restriction and the other provisions of this Section 2.7 shall go back into force. Nothing in this Section 2.7 shall relieve Loop of any of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed definition of "Authorized Customer" now includes potential customers, which contradicts the original definition tied to a Transferred Contract. This directly impacts Section 2.7, the "Marketing and Sale Restriction," because the restriction is intended to apply only to customers with Transferred Contracts. Expanding the definition to include any customer or potential customer introduces ambiguity about who the Joint Venture Company is restricted from marketing to, creating a strong in-text contradiction. The change removes the original constraint, making the section ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Acceptance of Proposed Contracts. All Eligible Contracts shall be deemed accepted by the Joint Venture Company and become a Transferred Contract on the date Loop delivers the Proposed Contract Notice for such Eligible Contract to the Joint Venture Company. In the event that the Proposed Contract Notice is a Considered Contract, the Joint Venture Company will have the right to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract within ten (10) days following Joint Venture Company's receipt of the Proposed Contract Notice (the "Review Period"). Joint Venture Company shall provide Loop with a written acceptance or rejection of each Considered Contract ("Acceptance Notice" or "Rejection Notice," as applicable) within the Review Period, which acceptance or rejection shall be in Joint Venture Company's sole discretion. If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed rejected by Joint Venture Company. Loop shall be entitled to present any rejected Considered Contract (whether deemed rejected or by receipt of a Rejection Notice) to an Alternate Producer on the same terms and conditions as those presented to Joint Venture Company. If Joint Venture Company provides an Acceptance Notice to Loop with respect to a Considered Contract during the Review Period, that Considered Contract shall become a Transferred Contract on the date Joint Venture Company provides that Acceptance Notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Acceptance of Proposed Contracts. All Eligible Contracts shall be deemed accepted by the Joint Venture Company and become a Transferred Contract on the date Loop delivers the Proposed Contract Notice for such Eligible Contract to the Joint Venture Company. In the event that the Proposed Contract Notice is a Considered Contract, the Joint Venture Company will have the right to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract; however, the Joint Venture Company's acceptance shall not be unreasonably withheld. Joint Venture Company shall provide Loop with a written acceptance or rejection of each Considered Contract ("Acceptance Notice" or "Rejection Notice," as applicable) within the Review Period, which acceptance or rejection shall be subject to Loop's prior approval. If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed accepted by Joint Venture Company. Loop shall not be entitled to present any rejected Considered Contract (whether deemed rejected or by receipt of a Rejection Notice) to an Alternate Producer on the same terms and conditions as those presented to Joint Venture Company. If Joint Venture Company provides an Acceptance Notice to Loop with respect to a Considered Contract during the Review Period, that Considered Contract shall only become a Transferred Contract upon Loop's approval.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces conflicting conditions regarding the acceptance of 'Considered Contracts'. Originally, the Joint Venture Company had sole discretion in accepting or rejecting these contracts. The modified version introduces the need for Loop's prior approval for the Joint Venture Company's acceptance, and changes the Joint Venture Company's discretion to 'not be unreasonably withheld', this introduces an in-text contradiction with the original clause. Additionally, it states that failure to provide any notice within the Review Period results in automatic acceptance, contradicting the original rejection. This changes creates an uncertainty on the obligations of the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.3 Acceptance of Proposed Contracts. All Eligible Contracts shall be deemed accepted by the Joint Venture Company and become a Transferred Contract on the date Loop delivers the Proposed Contract Notice for such Eligible Contract to the Joint Venture Company. In the event that the Proposed Contract Notice is a Considered Contract, the Joint Venture Company will have the right to review and determine whether to accept or reject the Considered Contract; however, the Joint Venture Company's acceptance shall not be unreasonably withheld. Joint Venture Company shall provide Loop with a written acceptance or rejection of each Considered Contract ("Acceptance Notice" or "Rejection Notice," as applicable) within the Review Period, which acceptance or rejection shall be subject to Loop's prior approval. If Joint Venture Company does not provide an Acceptance Notice or Rejection Notice to Loop within the Review Period, the Considered Contract will be deemed accepted by Joint Venture Company. Loop shall not be entitled to present any rejected Considered Contract (whether deemed rejected or by receipt of a Rejection Notice) to an Alternate Producer on the same terms and conditions as those presented to Joint Venture Company. If Joint Venture Company provides an Acceptance Notice to Loop with respect to a Considered Contract during the Review Period, that Considered Contract shall only become a Transferred Contract upon Loop's approval.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces multiple in-text contradictions. First, it changes the Joint Venture Company's 'sole discretion' to accept or reject Considered Contracts to a requirement that its acceptance 'not be unreasonably withheld', and that Loop's prior approval is required. This directly contradicts the original concept of sole discretion. Second, the modified text states that if the Joint Venture Company fails to provide notice within the Review Period, the contract is deemed accepted, whereas originally, the same lack of notice resulted in the contract being deemed rejected, as highlighted in Perturbation Explanation. Finally, the changed text restricts Loop from presenting rejected contracts to an Alternate Producer, contrary to the original text.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Confidential Information. The term "Confidential Information" means any information disclosed by one Party to the other (i) prior to the date of this Agreement but with respect to the subject matter of this Agreement, or (ii) pursuant to this Agreement, in each case which is in written, graphic, machine readable or other tangible form and is marked "Confidential," "Proprietary" or in some other manner to indicate its confidential nature. Confidential Information may also include oral information disclosed by one Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement, pro-vided that such information is designated as confidential at the time of disclosure and reduced to a written summary by the disclosing Party, within thirty (30) days after its oral disclosure, which is marked in a manner to indicate its confidential nature and delivered to the receiving party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Confidential Information. The term "Confidential Information" means any information disclosed by one Party to the other (i) prior to the date of this Agreement but with respect to the subject matter of this Agreement, or (ii) pursuant to this Agreement, in each case which is in written, graphic, machine readable or other tangible form. Confidential Information may also include oral information disclosed by one Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement, without any need for written summary or designation at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement for written, graphic, machine-readable forms to be marked as 'Confidential' or 'Proprietary'. Furthermore, it removes the requirement for oral information to be reduced to a written summary. This creates an in-text contradiction because the original definition specifies the necessity of marking written information and summarizing oral disclosures to qualify as confidential. In this version, information, under this section, could be interpreted as confidential regardless of being written or not. This introduces a contradiction in Article 4, specifically 4.2, regarding the obligations of confidentiality, as it's unclear what constitutes 'Confidential Information' and what procedures should be followed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Obligation. Each Party shall treat as confidential (as set forth herein) all Confidential Information of the other Party, and shall not use such Confidential Information except as contemplated in this Agreement or as otherwise authorized in writing. Each Party shall implement reasonable procedures to prohibit the unauthorized disclo-sure or misuse of the other Party's Confidential Information and shall not intentionally disclose such Confidential Information to any third party except as may be necessary or useful in connection with the rights and obligations of such Party under this Agreement, and subject to confidentiality obligations similar to those set forth in this Article 4. Each of the Parties shall use at least the same procedures and degree of care that it uses to prevent the disclosure of its own confidential information of like importance to prevent the disclosure of Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other Party under this Agreement, but in no event less than reasonable care.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for information to be marked as confidential or proprietary to be considered 'Confidential Information'. This directly contradicts section 4.2, which refers to 'Confidential Information (as set forth herein)' and outlines obligations based on that definition. The changed text broadens the scope of what can be considered confidential, creating ambiguity and potentially conflicting with the obligations outlined in 4.2, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.
